context.narrative: At 21:30 a scheduled Ansible run applied a 'standardize OSPF timers' playbook to all 40 campus routers. Within a minute, OSPF adjacencies started flapping campus-wide and inter-VLAN traffic began failing intermittently. The on-call engineer rolled nothing back yet — your job is to explain exactly what broke. Review the artifacts and state the root cause and the immediate fix.

artifacts.label: core-rtr1 syslog
artifacts.content:
Jul 17 21:31:12: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.2 on Gi0/1 from FULL to DOWN, Neighbor Down: Dead timer expired
Jul 17 21:31:13: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.2 on Gi0/1 from DOWN to INIT
Jul 17 21:31:53: %OSPF-5-ADJCHG: Process 1, Nbr 10.1.1.2 on Gi0/1 from INIT to DOWN

artifacts.label: playbook diff applied at 21:30 (git show, excerpt)
artifacts.content:
- name: standardize OSPF timers
  cisco.ios.ios_config:
    parents: interface {{ item }}
    lines:
      - ip ospf hello-interval 60
  loop: "{{ ospf_interfaces }}"
# intent per design doc: hello 10 / dead 40 everywhere
# NOTE: value '60' was copied from the dead-interval column of the design sheet

artifacts.label: core-rtr1: show ip ospf interface Gi0/1 (after run)
artifacts.content:
Timer intervals configured, Hello 60, Dead 240, Wait 240, Retransmit 5
  Neighbor Count is 1, Adjacent neighbor count is 0

artifacts.label: dist-sw2 (missed by the run - was unreachable): show ip ospf interface Vlan99
artifacts.content: Timer intervals configured, Hello 10, Dead 40, Wait 40, Retransmit 5

solution.explanation: The playbook pushed 'ip ospf hello-interval 60' (a value copied from the dead-interval column — the design intent was hello 10) to every reachable router. Devices the run missed (like dist-sw2, unreachable at 21:30) still run hello 10/dead 40, so every link between an updated and a non-updated router now has mismatched hello/dead timers — OSPF requires them to match, so those adjacencies cycle FULL→DOWN→INIT. Immediate fix: push hello-interval 10 back out (or remove the line) so both sides match.

context.narrative: Now repair the playbook so it applies the intended design (hello 10 / dead 40) to every OSPF interface. Fill in the missing config line that sets the correct hello interval (the dead interval defaults to 4x hello, matching the design).

artifacts.label: fix-ospf-timers.yml (your edit)
artifacts.content:
- name: apply design-standard OSPF timers
  cisco.ios.ios_config:
    parents: interface {{ item }}
    lines:
      - ____
  loop: "{{ ospf_interfaces }}"

artifacts.label: design doc excerpt
artifacts.content: OSPF timers (all campus links): hello-interval 10s, dead-interval 40s (default 4x hello)

solution.explanation: The line is 'ip ospf hello-interval 10' — with hello at 10s, the dead interval defaults to 40s (4x hello), matching the design and the untouched routers, so all adjacencies re-form.

stages.title: Stage 3 — Add the guardrail

context.narrative: The incident review asks for a pipeline guardrail so a bad timer value can never hit all 40 routers at once again. Which change is the most effective first guardrail?

artifacts.label: current CI pipeline
artifacts.content:
on merge to main:
  - ansible-playbook site.yml   # straight to all 40 routers, no check, no canary

choices: Run 'ansible-playbook --check --diff' against a canary group first, require human review of the diff, then roll out in batches
Increase the OSPF dead-interval everywhere so mismatches take longer to drop adjacencies
Switch the playbook from ios_config to raw so changes apply faster and the outage window is shorter
Add a cron job that reverts all OSPF config to a golden snapshot every night

acceptable_answers: run 'ansible-playbook --check --diff' against a canary group first, require human review of the diff, then roll out in batches
solution.explanation: Check mode + diff against a canary group surfaces exactly what would change (the wrong '60' would have been visible in review) before any production device is touched, and batched rollout caps the blast radius. The other options either mask the problem, speed up the damage, or fight the automation instead of gating it.
